chapter: Ch 11 — The Bedrock: commitments, hardness, and the discrete-log envelope
stratum: Group theory & number-theoretic hardness (where security lives)

phases.pre_rigorous: Back in Chapter 2 you met a sealed envelope: write a number on a slip, seal it, hand it over. Two promises came with it — the envelope HIDES the number until you open it, and once sealed you can't SWAP the slip. We never built the envelope; we only described it. Now we build it. Lead with the figure: a prime-order group drawn as a clock face, with two public marks g and h on the rim. To commit to a message m, you also roll a secret die r, and you place a single point on the clock: that point is your sealed envelope. The picture shows the magic. Keep the message m fixed and just re-roll r: the point jumps all around the clock — over every possible roll it lands on every position equally often. So from the sealed point alone, an onlooker cannot tell which message you started from: the die has smeared it across the whole ring. That is HIDING, and you can see it. Now the other promise: for a fixed message and a fixed die, there is exactly ONE point. To later claim a different message, you'd have to find a second die that lands on the very same point — and the only way to do that is to know the secret relationship between the two marks g and h, which nobody does. That is BINDING. One picture, both promises: a die that smears the message everywhere (hiding) yet pins down a single point per (message, die) pair (binding).
phases.rigorous: Now earn it. Work in a cyclic group of PRIME order q (concretely the order-q = 1019 subgroup of (Z/pZ)* for the safe prime p = 2039, so every non-identity element generates). Fix two generators g and h; crucially, h = g^a for some a = log_g(h) that NOBODY knows. The Pedersen commitment to a message m in Z/qZ with randomness r in Z/qZ is C = Com(m, r) = g^m * h^r. HIDING (perfect, information-theoretic). Fix any m. As r ranges over Z/qZ, the term h^r ranges over the entire group, so r |-> g^m * h^r is a bijection onto the group: C is uniformly distributed and statistically independent of m. No assumption is used — an unbounded adversary still learns nothing. Equivalently, for ANY target message m' there is exactly one r' = r + (m - m')*a^{-1} mod q with g^{m'} * h^{r'} = C, so C is consistent with every message. This destroys the bad intuition 'g^m leaks m': the blinding factor smears it over the whole group. BINDING (computational, == discrete log). Suppose an adversary outputs two valid openings (m, r) != (m', r') of the same C. Then g^m h^r = g^{m'} h^{r'}, so g^{m - m'} = h^{r' - r}, giving log_g(h) = a = (m - m') * (r' - r)^{-1} mod q (and r' != r whenever m != m'). Thus producing two openings is exactly solving the discrete log of h base g. Under the Discrete Logarithm assumption no efficient adversary can, so binding holds against bounded adversaries only — it is computational. This kills three bad intuitions at once: hiding does NOT rest on DLog (binding does); g and h are NOT arbitrary (their unknown relative log IS the binding security); and 'only computational' is not weakness — it is precisely the chapter's hardness bet. Concretely: g = 9, h = 726, m = 42, r = 800 give C = 71. The alternate opening m' = 100 forces r' = 800 + (42-100)*571^{-1} mod 1019 = 186, and g^100 * h^186 = 71 = C (perfect hiding). Feeding those two openings to the extractor returns a = (42-100)*(186-800)^{-1} mod 1019 = 571 = log_g(h) (binding == DLog).
phases.post_rigorous: Rebuild the intuition on the rigor, both halves at once. The clock that 'smears the message everywhere' IS the bijection r |-> g^m * h^r onto the whole group — perfect hiding is the statement that this map is a uniform shuffle, needing no assumption. The 'single point per (m, r), and you can't fake a second die' IS the extractor: a second opening hands you log_g(h), which DLog says you cannot have. Pair each picture with its statement and the asymmetry becomes inevitable: hiding is unconditional because it is a counting fact about a finite group; binding is conditional because it is a hardness fact about that group. You cannot make both perfect for one element — the very alternate opening that proves perfect hiding is what an unbounded adversary would use to break binding. This is the construction that finally builds Ch 2's sealed envelope, and it is the base of a whole line: stack many generators and you get the VECTOR Pedersen commitment h^r * prod g_i^{m_i}, whose succinct opening is the inner-product argument (IPA / Bulletproofs), and whose additive homomorphism (Com(m1,r1)*Com(m2,r2) = Com(m1+m2, r1+r2)) powers range proofs and proof-of-reserves. Keep one boundary sharp: this is a DISCRETE-LOG, transparent, additively-homomorphic commitment — perfectly hiding, computationally binding. It is NOT a KZG/pairing commitment (computationally binding under q-SDH, needing a trusted setup) — Ch 11 builds both, and conflating their assumptions and setups corrupts the security map.
phases.you_could_have_invented_this: Knowing only that you have a prime-order group with a generator g and can publish a second element h whose discrete log base g you don't know, you can invent the whole scheme: to seal m, roll random r and publish C = g^m * h^r. Hiding falls out by counting (h^r is uniform, so C hides m with no assumption); binding falls out by algebra (two openings give g^{m-m'} = h^{r'-r}, i.e. log_g(h), which you can't have). That is the Pedersen commitment — you derived it, you didn't receive it.

heuristic_rigorous_pairs: Re-roll the random die r and the sealed point jumps all around the clock, hitting every position equally — so the point alone hides which message you started from.
For fixed m, the map r |-> g^m * h^r is a bijection of Z/qZ onto the group, so C is uniform and statistically independent of m: PERFECT (information-theoretic) hiding, no assumption.

For a fixed message and a fixed die there is exactly one sealed point; to claim a different message you'd need a second die hitting the same point, which needs the secret link between the two marks g and h.

Two openings (m, r) != (m', r') of one C give g^{m-m'} = h^{r'-r}, hence log_g(h) = (m-m')*(r'-r)^{-1} mod q: breaking BINDING == solving discrete log; binding is computational under the DLog assumption.
One promise (hiding) is free; the other (binding) is a bet — you can't make both unbreakable with a single sealed point.

Hiding is unconditional (a counting fact about a finite group); binding is conditional (a hardness fact). The same alternate opening r' = r + (m-m')*a^{-1} that proves perfect hiding is what an unbounded adversary would use to break binding — so both cannot be perfect.
Concretely: with marks g = 9 and h = 726 on the order-1019 clock, message 42 and die 800 seal the point 71; message 100 with die 186 seals the SAME point 71.
In the order-1019 subgroup of (Z/2039Z)*: g^42 * h^800 = 71 and g^100 * h^186 = 71 (Sage-verified), and the extractor on these two openings returns log_g(h) = 571.

claims.claim: The group is the order-q subgroup of (Z/pZ)* with p = 2039 a safe prime (p = 2q + 1) and q = 1019 prime, so the subgroup has prime order and every non-identity element is a generator.
claims.evidence: Sage: is_prime(2039) and is_prime(1019) and 2039 == 2*1019 + 1, all True. Independent recompute confirms p prime, q prime, p == 2q+1.

claims.claim: g = 9 and h = 726 are two generators of the order-1019 subgroup (each has multiplicative order exactly 1019), and the secret relation is h = g^a with a = log_g(h) = 571.
claims.evidence: Sage: g.multiplicative_order() == 1019 and h.multiplicative_order() == 1019, both True; discrete_log(h, g) = 571 == sage_manifest.true_dlog. g = 3^2 and h = g^571 by construction.

claims.claim: For message m = 42 and randomness r = 800, the Pedersen commitment Com(m, r) = g^m * h^r mod 2039 equals C = 71.
claims.evidence: Sage recipe emits commitment = 71. Independent recompute g^42 * h^800 mod 2039 = 71. The manim scene recomputes COMMITMENT in pure Python and also gets 71; validate_scene_values reports no drift.

claims.claim: PERFECT HIDING (numerical): for fixed m = 42, sweeping r over [0, 1, 800, 17, 999] gives five DISTINCT commitments [1641, 590, 71, 143, 834]; over all 1019 values of r the commitment is uniform over the whole subgroup.
claims.evidence: Sage: hiding_orbit = [1641, 590, 71, 143, 834], hiding_distinct = True (71 is C, since r = 800 is in the sample). Independent recompute: { g^42 * h^r : r in 0..1018 } has exactly 1019 distinct elements = q, so r -> Com(42, r) is a bijection onto the subgroup. C is uniform and independent of m: perfect, information-theoretic hiding.

claims.claim: PERFECT HIDING (alternate opening): the SAME commitment C = 71 opens to a DIFFERENT message m' = 100 with randomness r' = 186, where r' = r + (m - m')*a^{-1} mod q.
claims.evidence: Sage: r_alt = (800 + (42 - 100)*inverse_mod(571,1019)) mod 1019 = 186, and g^100 * h^186 mod 2039 = 71 = C; hiding_collision_ok = True. Independent recompute reproduces r_alt = 186 and C_alt = 71.

claims.claim: COMPUTATIONAL BINDING reduces to discrete log: the two openings (42, 800) and (100, 186) of C = 71 yield log_g(h) = (m - m')*(r' - r)^{-1} mod q = 571 = a; producing two openings == solving DLog.
claims.evidence: Sage: extracted_a = ((42 - 100)*inverse_mod((186-800) mod 1019, 1019)) mod 1019 = 571 = true_dlog; binding_extracts_dlog = True. Independent recompute returns 571. Binding holds only against adversaries who cannot compute a = log_g(h): computational, under the DLog assumption.
claims.ledger_ref: CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 11: 'Pedersen commitment Com(m,r)=g^m h^r (C56, deg 39) -- perfectly hiding, computationally binding (binding => DLog).'

claims.claim: Pedersen is perfectly hiding and only computationally binding (the two cannot both be perfect for one group element); dual parameters would flip which property is unconditional.
claims.source: lemma (hiding uniformity is information-theoretic; binding reduces to DLog) + Sage demonstrations above
claims.evidence: Hiding shown information-theoretically (bijection r -> Com(m,r), no assumption). Binding shown to reduce to DLog (extractor recovers a = 571). The perfect-hiding alternate opening (same C, different m') is the witness that binding cannot also be perfect. Consistent with the chapter-bible characterization.

scene_value_check.note: Empty list = no drift; every computed value the manim scene displays (p, q, g, h, m, r, C, hiding_distinct) matches the Sage manifest. The scene recomputes Com(m,r) in pure Python.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk of conflating 'perfectly hiding' with 'perfectly binding' (claiming Pedersen is unconditionally secure on both axes).
adversarial_findings.resolution: Stated and proved the asymmetry: hiding is information-theoretic (bijection r -> Com(m,r), no assumption); binding is computational (extractor reduces two openings to log_g(h) = 571). The perfect-hiding alternate opening (m'=100, r'=186, same C=71) is the witness that binding cannot also be perfect. Verified in Sage; draft.md keeps the halves separate.

adversarial_findings.finding: Possible off-by-one / wrong-modulus error: exponents are mod q = 1019 while group elements are mod p = 2039.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Confirmed group multiplication is mod p = 2039 and ALL exponent arithmetic (a^{-1}, r_alt, the extractor) is mod q = 1019. Re-derived r_alt = 186 and extracted a = 571 two independent ways; both agree and C_alt = C = 71.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk that g or h is not a true generator (identity or order < q).
adversarial_findings.resolution: Verified g = 9 and h = 726 each have order exactly 1019 and != 1. Since q is prime and p = 2q+1 is safe, every quadratic residue != 1 has order q; g = 3^2 and h = g^571 are bona fide generators.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk that the figure or animation asset is empty, stale, or mismatched with the manifest.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Re-ran the recipe; assets/figures/pedersen-commitment.svg is 13,971 bytes, copied into figures/. Rendered the clip with render_scene(still=False); animations/pedersen-commitment.mp4 is 571,842 bytes. validate_scene_values returns [] (no drift).

comprehension_set.question: Write down the Pedersen commitment to a message m with randomness r in a prime-order group with public generators g and h, and name its two security properties and which one is unconditional.
comprehension_set.answer_key: Com(m, r) = g^m * h^r in the group. It is PERFECTLY (information-theoretically) HIDING and COMPUTATIONALLY BINDING. Hiding is the unconditional one: it needs no assumption. Binding rests on the discrete-log assumption between g and h.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'A commitment can be both perfectly hiding and perfectly binding' — anchored by naming exactly one property (hiding) as unconditional and the other (binding) as computational.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: the Discrete Logarithm assumption (given g and h = g^a in a prime-order group, recovering a is infeasible)

comprehension_set.question: In the order-1019 subgroup of (Z/2039Z)* with g = 9, h = 726, you publish C = g^42 * h^800 = 71. Does C leak the message m = 42? Exhibit a different message m' = 100 that the SAME C also opens to, and give the randomness r' that does it.
comprehension_set.answer_key: C does not leak m: for the fixed message, r -> g^42 * h^r is a bijection onto all 1019 group elements, so C = 71 is uniform and independent of m. The same C = 71 opens to m' = 100 with r' = r + (m - m')*a^{-1} mod 1019, where a = log_g(h) = 571; this gives r' = 186, and g^100 * h^186 mod 2039 = 71. (Knowing a is what makes such an alternate opening computable — see binding.)
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'C = g^m * h^r leaks m because m is in the exponent' — refuted by the bijection/uniformity and by an explicit second opening of the same C.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: modular arithmetic and modular inverses in Z/pZ and Z/qZ

comprehension_set.question: Suppose an adversary produces two valid openings (m, r) and (m', r') of the SAME commitment C with m != m'. What have they necessarily computed, and what does this tell you about the assumption binding rests on — and why is hiding NOT in the same boat?
comprehension_set.answer_key: From g^m h^r = g^{m'} h^{r'} you get g^{m-m'} = h^{r'-r}, hence log_g(h) = (m - m') * (r' - r)^{-1} mod q. So two openings force the discrete log of h base g: breaking binding == solving DLog, which is why binding is only computational. Hiding is different: it is proved by counting (r -> Com(m,r) is uniform over the group), needs no hardness assumption, and holds even against an unbounded adversary — that is why it is perfect.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'Hiding rests on the discrete-log assumption, just like binding' — separated by deriving binding from DLog while proving hiding by an unconditional uniformity argument.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: the Discrete Logarithm assumption (given g and h = g^a in a prime-order group, recovering a is infeasible)

comprehension_set.question: You could have invented this. You want to seal a number m so that (a) the sealed value reveals nothing about m, yet (b) you cannot later claim a different m. You have a prime-order group with a generator g, and you can publish a second element h whose discrete log base g you do not know. Derive a commitment from scratch and argue both properties.
comprehension_set.answer_key: Pick fresh randomness r and publish C = g^m * h^r. HIDING: for fixed m, as r ranges over the group's exponents, h^r is uniform over the whole group, so C is uniform and independent of m — the seal reveals nothing (no assumption needed). BINDING: if you could open C to two different messages (m, r) and (m', r'), then g^{m-m'} = h^{r'-r}, so you would know log_g(h) = (m-m')*(r'-r)^{-1}; since nobody knows that discrete log, you cannot. You have rediscovered the Pedersen commitment: perfectly hiding, computationally binding, with binding == discrete log.
comprehension_set.targets_stuck_point: 'g and h can be any two elements; their relationship does not matter' — the rediscovery hinges on publishing an h whose discrete log base g is unknown, which IS the binding security.
comprehension_set.on_miss_route_to: prime-order groups (why every non-identity element is a generator; safe primes p = 2q + 1)
